speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 2. Amy Godin interviewing Lisa Murik in Silver Spring, Maryland, USA. Today's date is November 10th, 1995. We were talking about the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who guarded the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. They were right by the gate, and they-- that's the only way you could go out is through the gate. There was no other entrance or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any opportunities to leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no way. When you came back from work, you just stayed in there, and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who lived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and father and my two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about your living space when you went to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was another family in the same house. And it was very small space. Like, me and my two sisters were in one room, and my mother and father was in another room. And we shared the kitchen, and the eating area was shared with other family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a bathroom in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We hardly have any real, good food. Whatever ration we got of food, that's what we had to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that you were observant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about your observance of Judaism when you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was very hard to have it, that kind of, you know, observance because you couldn't go no synagogue. And really, you didn't know what day was a holiday. It was very hard. You just lived day by day, and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned to me in the pre-interview that one of your hobbies was reading. Were you were able to read any books?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I couldn't. Nothing. Get up in the morning. Go to work, and came back at night. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 16, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have work permits?

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't need no work permits. Everybody had to-- young and old had to go in to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they have a couple people assigned to their office. They knew more exactly who was-- you know, how many people were in the ghetto and all that. They had the list of people, and they just picked up people to go to work every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody that worked for the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you didn't. You didn't have to know people, you know. They just came to the ghetto, and they just get us together. And we went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people from other places in the Ostrog ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Refugees from other areas.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no refugee center.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any knowledge, when you were in the ghetto, of what was going on outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that the Germans came and rounded up people. Do you remember any of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just remember seeing the truck and [INAUDIBLE] few Germans just rounded up young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any radios or newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing. OK. Could you tell me about your plans to escape the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you couldn't have really made plans to escape because you wore this yellow circle on your clothes. And wherever you worked, you had to wear that circle. And they knew exactly who you were.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Getting to the point when you were in hiding--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in your home, you mentioned earlier, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how long had you planned to have a hiding place in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we went into hiding, we didn't plan to stay there forever. Because my mother figured, you know, we had another place to go where we could hide till we be liberated, so we just planned to stay for a few days, like, till it got quiet with the Germans. And then we could leave and go to another hiding. And then we got out earlier than we wanted to because of the water problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When you left the hiding place in your home--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the other house, and then we went to the Christian people who didn't accept us. And then we went to another place where they kept us overnight. And the next day, we went to outskirts of the city. And there we couldn't be accepted, so then my mother went and found a place. And that's where we stayed for 16 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the people's name who hid-- who helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman who hid us was-- her name was Horpina Zygadlo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her first name-- I think-- is spelled H-O-R-P-I-N-A. Then the last name is Z-Y-G-A-D-L-O. I think that's how you spell it. It's hard from Polish to, you know, American spelling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that located?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was outskirts of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you describe the hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a very small place. And we had just-- no, no blankets, nothing. No pillows, just straw-- and that's how we sat there and lived there for the 16 months. The woman brought us food once a day. Early in the morning, she brought us potatoes. Which every one of us had one potato and a small bottle of water. And that's how we lived for 16 months, with that food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, mother, and my two sisters. Then when she had bread-- she used to bring us a piece of bread-- we didn't ate that bread the same day. What my father did, he took a rope-- a little rope-- and he tied the bread to the pole so in case she couldn't come the next day with food, we had the bread. And it was very hard to, you know, to live like that. No, we had no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that room that you were in indoors or out of doors?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was an underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you were in that room? What was a day like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that hole, you mean? We sat there, and we talked and just tried to go by day by day and wait for the minute. Maybe a miracle will happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one miracle did happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a summer day. And the woman, when nobody was in the field, she took the-- there was a pumpkin on the opening. She removed the pumpkin so we could get some air. And while we were sitting and talking, didn't take maybe 10 minutes, a car came by and throw the pumpkin back on the opening. I was ready to go push it back, and my father said, no, let's see what happens. And sure enough, two minutes later, two German soldiers passed by. And it looked like the car knew that we were there, and she had to put that pumpkin back on that hole. And that was a, a miracle in itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not-- we were five people in there. And we only could-- if we laid down, we could only have our legs under us. We couldn't stretch them out. It was very small.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever able to go outside the hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, once we had to get out of there because it was wintertime. It was very cold, and she thought-- the woman who kept us-- she thought that somebody saw us. So we went out in the evening from there. We laid in the woods on the ice all night. And next morning, we went back in. That's the only time we went out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the 16 months that you were there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Did you have any contact with any other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- with nobody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only thing-- she couldn't read and write, so she used to go to town and bring a newspaper. And in the evening when it got dark, she came with a candle, and my father read the news to her. That's the only information we had about what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were able to find out what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, through the paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did she bring the paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came a, a couple times a week with the paper. And also, my parents told her, she hears something-- what's going on, the Germans are leaving-- to let us know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We been in that hole till '45, winter '45-- 16 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to have any religious observance--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know when the holiday was. We didn't know nothing. We had no calendar. We didn't-- no books, nothing. So just life was going on just day to day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody else that helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To hide?

speaker: interviewer
text: Um-hum.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, her husband didn't like it, what she was doing. And he told her he might just go ahead and report us. And she was so nice. And she said to him if he says anything that she's hiding out Jewish people, she'd take a butcher knife and cut his throat. She threatened him. And he kept quiet. She had two kids with him. The little boy was four years old, and he-- they-- he knew that we were there, and-- because he used to go in the fields and sometimes pull out some carrots from the ground, some vegetables, bring it to us. And he used to say that the Germans would not live the day to kill us. A kid, four years old-- he understood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other instances that happened that you could describe?

speaker: interviewee
text: While we were hiding?

speaker: interviewer
text: Um-hum.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing much, except that one incident with the car, which, you know-- oh, yes, there was another time. She was sitting and my father was reading the paper, and she noticed some people walking. So she started-- she blew the candle out, and she started throwing the pumpkins on top of-- you know, here and there. And she was talking to herself that her cow was sick and she has to find a good pumpkin. And what happened, we heard those two people walk over to her and ask of her for directions. And then they left. And she left, and she didn't came back no more over the paper because she was afraid in case there's people watching her. That's only two times when this happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after that point, your father never read the newspaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She came back. Then she came-- one day she came, and she was saying that the Russians are coming in, and the Germans were leaving. And it was very good to hear the news, because we knew we'd be able get out and be free.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, my mother waited some-- gave some time. And one day, it was [INAUDIBLE] time. And my mother and father, they left us behind in the hole. And they said they will go to the city and see what's happening. Because in case something goes wrong, they can come back faster by themselves than with all, you know, all of us. So they left us three behind, and they both went into the city. And passed by a couple days till they came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day, was a big snowstorm and covered us up so bad. When they came back to get us, they couldn't find us. And we, we heard walking. You know, you know, I heard down in the bottom. Now, I didn't know who was walking. I didn't know if it was my parents or was Germans. You never know, because you cannot see nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were knocking with the shovels, and we were sitting so quiet. We were afraid to even move. And then all of sudden, my mother said in Jewish, children, where are you? So when I heard her voice, I knew they came for us. So I had a little stick down the bottom, I poked up, and they took us out. And they came with a horse and wagon to pick us up with some clothes. And they took us to the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my younger sister, she couldn't walk at all. She was so weak. So one of the doctors took her into their-- this doctor took her on into his house and kept her to put her back on her feet. And another doctor took us in till we got on, back on our feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how you got liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians came back. Now, we didn't stay long in our-- well, our house was burned down. So we find a empty house. And we went into that house, and we occupied that house. And whoever was liberated, was the first stop at mother's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the house that you're talking about, was that your house inside the ghetto or out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- because that time, no fence was no more. And you couldn't tell where really the house was, if it was before in the ghetto or was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time of the year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the wintertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- of what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stayed there for a while. And at that time, my brother-- who's not really my brother-- came to us. The way he came to us, he worked at a farmer's, you know, place. He had a farm. And the man knew my mother. The man from the farm, he was Christian. He came one day. And he told my mother that he has a young man, and he would like to give him up. So my mother told him to bring him over, and she will take him in. So he brought him over. He was by that time maybe 13 years old-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 13 or 14, something like that. And at first the young man didn't want to stay with us because he only knew the farmer. So my mother told him that-- not to be afraid. He couldn't speak Jewish nor Polish. He, he spoke Russian. And just to try it out. So he decided to, to stay with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Mike. And he-- we didn't really have enough furniture in the house where we could put him to sleep. So me and my sisters gave up our bed, and we slept on the floor in the same room to watch him so he wouldn't run away from us. And he got used to us, and then he told us how he got liberated. What happened, he and his mother and father and his sister-- and they took the Jews in his hometown together. And they brought them to one place where was already-- a grave was already digged out. And before they started shooting, he-- the soldier didn't look. He crawled away into the bushes, and he saw with his own eyes his parents get killed with his sister. And he wandered from city to city, farm, farm till finally he find that place where to stay. So-- and then he asked my mother if she wouldn't mind not to adopt him and change his name, because he would like to carry on his own name. And my mother, you know, she told him, fine. You've got your wish, and he stayed with us. Then he find a uncle in Chicago, and he left. At that time, I was already here in Washington. And he left for his uncle, and he was in touch with me. He would let us know that. And he didn't like the way his uncle was treating him. He wrote me a letter one day. He wanted to come back and be with us. And I said, fine. And he lived together with us till he got married. And he treated my mother and father just like his own parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Getting back to your liberation, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was it like when you left your hiding place and--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very strange. Everything looked, you know, funny to you because you were away from the war. Like, everything was like you just were born. And it was very hard to get used to back to normal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find a home and-- you mentioned you had furniture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the houses were empty because most of the houses which were empty were Jewish houses. So we walked in one house. There was furniture. There was a bed, you know, table and-- so I really give credit, most of it, to my mother. She was a very strong woman, a very good woman. Her door, even before the war, was always open to people. She did a lot of charity work. And I really want to thank her that I'm alive today. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what did you do? Did you go to work? Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: After we got liberated?

speaker: interviewer
text: Um-hum.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't stay in Ostrog long. We stayed awhile, then we left for Lodz. My mother made a little business. Because the stores were empty, you could take a store. And at first, my father was working, you know, for the Russians before we went to Lodz. And they saved up a little money. She opened up a little business. And that's how we made our living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: They both worked in the store. And then we left. From there, we went to Germany, to a DP camp from Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like when you lived in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a, a normal life every day. I didn't-- I worked in my mother's store. And that's where I met my husband-- in Lodz-- and I got married. And after I got married, we left Lodz, and we went to Germany. And we were in a DP camp in Germany, in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you leave Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left in 1949. No. We left before. We left 1945. We went to Germany, and my daughter, my older daughter, was born in the DP camp in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What types of challenges did you face when you were in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there still anti-Semitism going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was anti-Semitism, you could say. Then was more than before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was even more than before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what types of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't like the Jewish people, for once.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the only thing I could remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What was it like when you arrived in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they have barracks. Each family have, you know, a, a, you know, a couple of rooms with a little kitchen. And we, we had received ration food from the American people. We were on the American side, and we stayed there for-- till '49. And in '49, we came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and father, my two sisters, and my husband. And then my daughter was born there. My daughter was with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Different kind of work. It's hard to remember. It's been so long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism in the DP camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because there, there were only Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned-- what was the name of the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Schlachtensee-- this city, you know, outskirts of Berlin, Schlachtensee. The camp, I'm sorry. I don't remember the name. Now  I know it was Schlachtensee.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is hard. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what happened in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, life went on. People worked. And we all-- you know, people started looking for relatives in the United States. And I don't remember exactly how my husband found his relatives here in Washington. You know, there was the HIAS, and there was the Joint who took care of us in the DP camp. And through them, you'd find your relatives here. And whoever, you know, found relatives, they could come to the States. I was the first-- I, my husband, and my daughter-- we were the first to leave Germany to come here. And we left in 1949, March 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the DP camp, was there any religious observance?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [BACKROUND NOISE] You mentioned that you had a child in the DP camp. What was your child's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Barbara. And she was two and a half when I came here to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
